questions.question: The 'Monsoon' word is derived from which language?
questions.explanation: The word 'monsoon' is derived from the Arabic word 'mausim' meaning season or seasonal wind.

questions.question: The Rann of Kutch is a seasonal salt marsh located in:
questions.explanation: The Rann of Kutch is a vast seasonal salt marsh in Gujarat, India, and Pakistan. It is the world's largest salt desert.

questions.question: India's climate is classified as:
questions.explanation: India has a tropical monsoon climate (Koppen: Am/Aw/Bsh). The word 'monsoon' comes from Arabic 'mausim' (season). The four seasons: Cold weather (Dec-Feb), Hot weather (Mar-May), Southwest monsoon (Jun-Sep), Retreating monsoon (Oct-Nov).

questions.question: The wettest place in India (and one of the wettest in the world) is:
questions.explanation: Mawsynram (11,873mm/year average) and Cherrapunji/Sohra (11,777mm/year) in Meghalaya receive the highest rainfall in India and among the highest in the world. This is due to orographic (relief) rainfall as moist Bay of Bengal winds hit the Khasi Hills.

questions.question: El Niño affects Indian monsoon by:
options: Weakening the southwest monsoon, often leading to deficient rainfall/drought in India
questions.explanation: El Niño (warming of central/eastern Pacific Ocean) weakens the Walker Circulation, suppresses Indian monsoon convection, and typically leads to below-normal monsoon rainfall in India — causing droughts. La Niña has the opposite effect (stronger monsoon).

questions.question: Laterite soil is formed in regions with:
options: High temperature and high seasonal rainfall — leaching removes silica, iron and aluminium oxides remain
questions.explanation: Laterite soils form in hot, humid tropical regions with high seasonal rainfall (alternating wet-dry seasons). Leaching removes silica and bases; iron and aluminium oxides remain, giving red/brick-like colour. Found in Tamil Nadu hill stations, Kerala, Karnataka, Odisha, Assam.

questions.question: The 'Kharif crops' are sown during:
options: June-July (beginning of southwest monsoon) — harvested October-November
questions.explanation: Kharif crops are sown at the start of the southwest monsoon (June-July) and harvested in Oct-Nov. Examples: rice, cotton, jowar (sorghum), bajra, groundnut, sugarcane, jute. Rabi crops are sown Oct-Nov, harvested March-April (wheat, mustard, barley).

questions.question: Tamil Nadu's main crop and what it depends on:
options: Rice, depends primarily on northeast monsoon (Oct-Dec) and Kaveri/other rivers
questions.explanation: Rice is Tamil Nadu's main crop. Tamil Nadu's agriculture depends heavily on the northeast monsoon (Oct-Dec) unlike most of India (which depends on southwest monsoon). The Kaveri delta (Thanjavur) is the 'Rice Bowl of Tamil Nadu'.

questions.question: Vedanthangal Bird Sanctuary in Tamil Nadu is famous for:
options: Water birds (Painted stork, Open-billed stork, Cormorants, Egrets) — Asia's oldest bird sanctuary
questions.explanation: Vedanthangal (Chengalpattu district) is Asia's oldest and one of India's most important bird sanctuaries. It hosts thousands of migratory and resident water birds during the breeding season (Nov-March). Established c. 1798 by local farmers.

questions.question: Tamil Nadu receives most of its rainfall from:
questions.explanation: Tamil Nadu is unique — it receives about 60% of its annual rainfall from the Northeast (Retreating) monsoon during October-December. This is why Tamil Nadu gets rain when most other states have post-monsoon dry weather.

questions.question: The southwest monsoon arrives in India first in:
questions.explanation: The southwest monsoon arrives in Kerala around 1 June each year and then progresses northward. Kerala is the first Indian State to receive the southwest monsoon.

questions.question: Western Disturbances bring winter rainfall to:
options: North India (Punjab, Haryana, UP, Jammu & Kashmir)
questions.explanation: Western Disturbances are cyclonic systems from the Mediterranean Sea that travel eastward, bringing winter rainfall to North India (especially Punjab, Haryana, UP) — beneficial for Rabi crops (wheat).

questions.question: The retreat of the southwest monsoon begins from:
questions.explanation: The southwest monsoon retreats (withdraws) from India starting from Rajasthan/northwestern India in September, and completely withdraws from India by early December (it retreats southward).

questions.question: El Niño phenomenon is associated with:
options: Unusual warming of the central and eastern Pacific Ocean
questions.explanation: El Niño is a climate pattern where the central and eastern Pacific Ocean warms unusually, disrupting global weather. It typically causes drought in India (weak monsoon), flooding in South America, and other global effects.

questions.question: The Paris Agreement (2015) aims to limit global warming to:
questions.explanation: The Paris Agreement (COP21, December 2015) aims to hold global average temperature increase to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels and pursue efforts to limit it to 1.5°C.

questions.question: 'Isotherms' connect places having:
questions.explanation: Isotherms are lines on a map connecting places having the same mean temperature at a given time or for a given period. They help visualize temperature patterns and climate zones.

questions.question: The Rann of Kutch is a unique geographical feature because:
options: It is a large salt marsh/seasonal lake (dry in summer, flooded in monsoon)
It is the largest desert in India
questions.explanation: The Rann of Kutch (Gujarat) is a large seasonal salt marsh — dry and salty in summer (white expanse), and partially flooded during monsoon. It covers ~23,000 sq km. Famous for the Wild Ass Sanctuary and Rann Utsav.

questions.question: The main Indian Ocean monsoon system is driven by:
options: Differential heating of land and sea causing seasonal reversal of winds
questions.explanation: The Indian monsoon is driven by differential heating — land heats faster than the sea. In summer, low pressure over the hot Indian landmass draws moisture-laden winds from the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal, causing rain.

questions.question: The Andaman and Nicobar Islands experience heavy rainfall because:
options: They receive both southwest and northeast monsoon rains
They are near the equator only
questions.explanation: Andaman and Nicobar Islands receive rainfall from both the southwest monsoon (May-October) and the northeast monsoon (November-December), giving them about 300 cm of annual rainfall. They are near the equator.

questions.question: Which Indian state produces the most cotton?
questions.explanation: Gujarat is India's largest cotton producer (about 35% of India's cotton). Maharashtra (Vidarbha) is second. India is the world's largest cotton producer. Cotton requires black cotton soil and moderate rainfall.

questions.question: The Atacama Desert in South America (Chile/Peru) is the driest because:
options: Rain shadow effect of Andes + cold Humboldt current causing subsiding dry air
questions.explanation: Atacama is the world's driest non-polar desert due to: (1) rain shadow of the Andes blocking Atlantic moisture, and (2) cold Humboldt current stabilizing the atmosphere, preventing convective rainfall.

questions.question: The largest salt lake in India is:
questions.explanation: Sambhar Lake (Rajasthan) is India's largest inland saltwater lake (about 230 sq km during monsoon, much smaller in summer). It supplies most of Rajasthan's salt. Chilika is brackish, not fully saline.

questions.question: The 'Convergence Zone' where trade winds from both hemispheres meet is called:
questions.explanation: The ITCZ (Intertropical Convergence Zone) is a band near the equator where northeast and southeast trade winds converge. It plays a major role in the Indian monsoon mechanism.

questions.question: Kodaikanal hill station is known for:
options: Star-shaped lake, shola forests, and pleasant climate
Being highest hill station in South India
questions.explanation: Kodaikanal (Princess of Hill Stations) in the Palani Hills, Tamil Nadu, is known for its star-shaped artificial lake, shola forests, grasslands, and pleasant climate. Silver Cascade waterfall is nearby.

questions.question: The Dzukou Valley is in:
questions.explanation: Dzukou Valley (2,452 m) on the Nagaland-Manipur border is known as the 'Valley of Flowers of the Northeast' — famous for its seasonal flowers, especially the Dzukou lily. It is a biodiversity-rich valley.

questions.question: The 'Jet Stream' is:
options: A narrow band of fast-moving air in the upper troposphere
questions.explanation: The Jet Stream is a narrow, fast-moving band of air (wind speed 100-300 km/h) in the upper troposphere/lower stratosphere at 7-16 km altitude. It influences weather patterns, aviation routes, and the Indian monsoon.

questions.question: What is the primary factor influencing India's climate?
questions.explanation: India's climate is primarily influenced by monsoon winds, which bring seasonal rainfall. This phenomenon is unique to the Indian subcontinent.

questions.question: Which mountain range runs along the western coast of India?
questions.explanation: The Western Ghats mountain range runs along the western coast of India, parallel to the Arabian Sea.

questions.question: What is the name of the river that flows through the city of Madurai?
questions.explanation: The Vaigai River flows through the city of Madurai in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which type of soil is commonly found in the delta regions of India?
questions.explanation: Alluvial soil is commonly found in the delta regions of India, which are formed by the deposition of sediment carried by rivers.

questions.question: What is the name of the strait that separates India from Sri Lanka?
questions.explanation: The Palk Strait separates India from Sri Lanka, connecting the Bay of Bengal to the Indian Ocean.

questions.explanation: Chennai is the largest port in Tamil Nadu, handling a significant volume of India's maritime trade.

questions.question: What is the average annual rainfall in India?
questions.explanation: India receives an average annual rainfall of around 1200 mm, with significant variation across different regions.

questions.question: Which river is considered the 'Ganga of the South'?
questions.explanation: The Cauvery River is considered the 'Ganga of the South' due to its cultural and spiritual significance in southern India.
